questions.question: Explain how system calls provide the interface between a running program and the operating system.
keyPoints: System call is the programmatic interface between user program and OS kernel
User program runs in user mode with restricted access
System call triggers a TRAP instruction causing mode switch to kernel mode
Kernel looks up system call number in the system call table
Appropriate handler function is executed in kernel mode
Result is returned and mode switches back to user mode
Draw the flow diagram showing user mode to kernel mode transition

questions.question: Describe the three methods of passing parameters to the operating system during a system call.
keyPoints: Registers: parameters placed in CPU registers, fastest but limited by register count
Block/Table: parameters stored in memory block, block address passed via register, used by Linux
Stack: parameters pushed onto stack by program, popped by OS, unlimited capacity

questions.question: Differentiate between API, system call, and system call interface.
keyPoints: API is a high-level set of functions for programmers (e.g., POSIX, Win32)
System call is the actual interface to the kernel for requesting OS services
System call interface is the layer that maps API calls to system calls using the system call table
Programmers use APIs for portability; APIs internally invoke system calls

questions.question: Trace the execution of the read() system call from a user program to the kernel and back.
keyPoints: Program calls read(fd, buffer, count)
C library sets up registers with system call number and parameters
TRAP/INT instruction executed, mode switches to kernel mode
Kernel reads system call number from register
System call table lookup finds sys_read handler
Kernel validates parameters and reads data from file
Data copied to user buffer, return value placed in register
Mode switches back to user mode, control returns to program
